title: Persistence: RDB vs AOF tradeoffs
description: RDB snapshots are compact with a larger loss window; AOF logs writes for durability at higher cost.
body: Use when deciding how durable Redis must be. RDB writes a periodic binary snapshot — small files, fast restore, but a crash loses every write since the last snapshot (often minutes). AOF appends each write command and replays it on restart; with appendfsync everysec the loss window is about a second, with always it is near zero but slower, and the file is larger and replays slower on boot (mitigated by AOF rewrite). Do not assume Redis is durable by default — a pure-cache config may have no persistence. Actions: enable AOF (often alongside RDB) when Redis holds data you cannot lose. Validation signals include a kill-restart test reloading the dataset within the chosen window. Risk boundary: RDB-only when Redis is a system of record risks minutes of silent data loss on crash.
